figures.caption: Table 11: The average SHD on the CPDAG learned over our ẑ and z w.r.t. the oracle CPDAG.

Figure 10: Example images of multiple ball dataset, modified based on the rendering code provided by Ahuja et al. (2022). Balls can move freely inside the frame, and each ball’s coordinates can be fixed randomly to a constant value, which causes their latent dimensions to be degenerate; Varying the number of balls from 2 to 6.

Figure 8: Ablation study on increasing the distance between masked value and mean of latents from 0.0σ to 3.0σ, where σ is the standard deviation of latents, and fixing n = 10, k = 1, m = 10, ρ = 50%, θ = 0. Disentangling the latent variables is more complicated in the case of a larger deviation from zero translation.

Figure 7: Piecewise linear mixing function with linear causal relation and Gaussian noise: ablation study on increasing the ratio of active (unmasked) variables ρ from 1 variable only to 75% and fixing n = 10, k = 1, m = 10, δ = 0.0σ, θ = 0. Learning the latent variables is more complicated in the case with a larger portion of active variables.

Table 1: Results for the numerical experiments. The bold font highlights the parameters that vary in each block. R2(Stage 1) shows identifiability up to AT after Stage 1 MCC(Stage 1) reflects the necessity of sparsity to achieve identifiability up to PS; MCC(Stage 2) presents the identifiability up to PS after Stage 2; MCC(VaDE) reports the results of Kivva et al. (2022). Full results with Std. Dev. are in Tab. 3.
Table 4: Results for the numerical experiments (Stage 2) tested on independent data. The bold font indicates which parameters are varying in each block of rows.

Figure 9: Ablation study on increasing the rotation of common basis from 0.0◦ to 45.0◦, and fixing n = 10, k = 1, m = 10, ρ = 50%, δ = 0.0σ. Disentangling the latent variables is more complicated in the case of a larger deviation from the standard basis.
Table 9: Results for the numerical experiments (Stage 1) on nondegenerate GMM data. The bold font indicates which parameters are varying in each block of rows.
Table 2: Parameters for experiments results in Sec. 5 and App. D.
Table 17: Results for the numerical experiments (Stage 2) on misspecified settings.

Figure 4: Ablation study on increasing the latent dimension n from 5 to 40 and fixing k = 1, m = 10, ρ = 50%, δ = 0.0σ, θ = 0. The case with a higher n is more complicated to learn the latent variables.

Table 3: Results for the numerical experiments (Average ± Standard Deviation). The bold font indicates which parameters are varying in each block of rows. R2(Stage 1) shows the identifiability up to AT after Stage 1 (Thm. 3.7); MCC(Stage 1) reflects the neccesarity of sparsity to achieve PTS identifiability; MCC(Stage 2) presents the identifiability up to PTS after Stage 2 (Thm. 3.9).

Table 12: Architecture of the convolutional autoencoder used in Sec. 5 (input size: 3 × 64 × 64).

Table 8: Ablation study without sparsity constraints and baselines: Average MCCs over 5 random seeds. Ours = proposed method, WO = without sparsity constraints, Oracle (Xu et al., 2024), VaDE (Kivva et al., 2022)

Figure 3: Visualization of Example B.15: f switches half axes of z1 and z2. The orange area refers to the support of component 1, and the green area refers to the support of component 2 before transformation.

Figure 5: Ablation study on increasing the density of causal graphs k from 0 to 3 and fixing n = 10, m = 10, ρ = 50%, δ = 0.0σ, θ = 0. In the case with a denser graph, it is more complicated to learn the latent variables.

Figure 6: Ablation study on increasing the number of Leaky-ReLU layers (m − 1) from 3 to 20 and fixing n = 10, k = 1, ρ = 50%, δ = 0.0σ, θ = 0. The case with a higher n is more complicated to learn the latent variables. The case with larger m is more complicated to learn the latent variables due to a greater extent of nonlinearity.
